summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that an unauthenticated attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript code on the FUXA server via the POST /api/runscript endpoint. The exploit sends a crafted JSON payload with script.test=true and arbitrary code in script.code, which is compiled and executed server-side by Module._compile().
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should cause the crafted script payload to be compiled and executed inside the FUXA server container, returning the proof string 'EVOHUNT-RCE-SUCCESS' in the HTTP response.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh downloaded the FUXA v1.2.10 source, installed dependencies, built and started the FUXA server, sent the crafted POST /api/runscript request, and received the response '"EVOHUNT-RCE-SUCCESS"' confirming arbitrary JavaScript was executed server-side.
verification_method: The exploit script sends JavaScript code that calls require('fs').writeFileSync() to write a proof string to a file and then reads it back via return. The verify.sh script checks that the server response contains the exact proof string 'EVOHUNT-RCE-SUCCESS', distinguishing real code execution from a generic parser error or startup failure.
evidence_produced_by_run: The FUXA server started successfully and responded to GET /api/version.
POST /api/runscript returned HTTP 200 with the response body '"EVOHUNT-RCE-SUCCESS"'.
The proof string was generated by server-side JavaScript execution using require('fs'), confirming full Node.js module access.
No authentication token was required because secureEnabled defaults to false.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh downloads the target repository archive from GitHub, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
